questions.question: Why can't you write `new T()` inside a generic class?
options: Generics only work with reference types
Type erasure removes type information at runtime, so the JVM doesn't know which constructor to call
T might not have a no-arg constructor
questions.explanation: Due to type erasure, T is replaced with Object at runtime — the JVM has no way to know the actual type. Additionally, there's no guarantee T has a no-arg constructor, so even if the type were known, instantiation might fail.

questions.question: What does PECS stand for and when do you use `? extends T`?
options: Producer Extends Consumer Super — use extends when producing (reading) values
Producer Extends Consumer Super — use extends when consuming (writing) values
Parameter Extends Class Super — use extends for class hierarchies
Polymorphic Extension Creates Subtypes — use extends for subtyping
questions.explanation: PECS = Producer Extends, Consumer Super. When a generic structure produces (provides) values you read, use ? extends T. When it consumes (accepts) values you write, use ? super T.

questions.question: Which is a valid bounded type parameter?
questions.explanation: Use 'extends' for both classes and interfaces in bounds (not 'implements' or 'super'). Multiple bounds are joined with '&'. You can have at most one class bound (listed first) plus multiple interface bounds. String is a final class so C is invalid.

questions.question: Can you add an Integer to a `List<? extends Number>`?
options: No — the compiler doesn't know the specific subtype, so only null can be added
Yes — but only if you cast to List<Number> first
questions.explanation: List<? extends Number> could be List<Double>, List<Integer>, etc. Adding an Integer to what might be a List<Double> would break type safety. The compiler prevents all adds except null.

questions.question: What is the type of `list.get(0)` when `list` is `List<? super Integer>`?
questions.explanation: With ? super Integer, the list could be List<Integer>, List<Number>, or List<Object>. The compiler can only guarantee the return type is Object, since that's the uppermost possibility.
